common_name: Echeveria (Hen and Chicks Succulent, Mexican Echeveria)

planting.note: Plant in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean, gritty soil in full sun to light shade; Echeveria requires excellent drainage above all else — more plants are lost to overwatering and wet winter soil than to cold. In zone 9, most Echeveria species tolerate brief light frosts to 28–30°F when well-established and dry.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, gritty soil in full sun; excellent drainage is the single most important requirement for Echeveria — the plants are extraordinarily tolerant of neglect, heat, and drought but will rot rapidly in waterlogged or continuously moist soil. Echeveria is the quintessential rosette-forming succulent of Mexican origin, encompassing over 150 species and hundreds of named hybrids and cultivars that form the backbone of the global succulent garden hobby — their perfect rosette geometry, frosted or wax-coated silvery-blue to green to purple-red leaves, and jewel-like appearance have made them the most photographed succulents in the world. The rosettes range from marble-sized miniatures to dinner-plate-sized giants; flower spikes rise 6–18 inches above the foliage in spring and summer with bell-shaped, orange, yellow, red, or pink flowers that attract hummingbirds. Echeveria propagates readily from leaf cuttings (leaves gently twisted off and left to callus, then placed on moist gritty soil), stem cuttings, and offsets (chicks) produced by the mother hen rosette. In humid Gulf Coast climates, Echeveria should be planted in raised beds or containers with excellent drainage; humidity-tolerant species like E. peacockii, E. imbricata, and E. runyonii perform better in coastal gardens than the most sensitive species. Outstanding as container specimens, in rock gardens, and in xeric landscape designs.
sources: Cactus and Succulent Society of America
San Diego Cactus and Succulent Society
University of California Cooperative Extension — Succulents for California

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, gritty soil in full sun; Elephant Bush tolerates brief temperatures to about 28–30°F when established and dry, making it useful for zone 9b–12 landscapes.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun to light shade; Portulacaria afra (Elephant Bush, Dwarf Jade, or Spekboom in Afrikaans) is a South African succulent shrub and small tree that is both one of the most ecologically important plants on earth and one of the most practical ornamental succulents for warm, dry landscapes. In its native South Africa's Eastern Cape, spekboom forms vast thicket ecosystems and is one of the most efficient carbon-sequestering plants known — Kruger National Park estimates that spekboom thicket sequesters 4–10 times more carbon per hectare than tropical rainforest. As a garden plant, Elephant Bush is valued for its ease of care, attractive small rounded succulent leaves (resembling jade plant Crassula ovata but faster-growing and more heat-tolerant), and its edible, pleasantly sour leaves used in South African salads and stews. The shrubs grow rapidly in warm conditions (up to 3 feet per year in zones 10–12), are extraordinarily drought-tolerant once established, and root easily from stem cuttings. The leaves are eaten by elephants (hence 'Elephant Bush'), baboons, and other wildlife in the wild. Excellent as a low-water landscape shrub, large container specimen, or informal hedge in zone 9b–12; root cuttings easily to propagate. The similar-looking variegated form (leaves edged in cream) is widely grown as a decorative specimen.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Succulents for California
Cactus and Succulent Society of America

common_name: Blue Chalk Sticks (Blue Senecio, Senecio mandraliscae)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; Blue Chalk Sticks spreads vigorously by stem rooting and becomes an effective ground cover in sunny, well-drained areas of zone 9–12 landscapes.

directions: Plant rooted stem cuttings in very well-drained, lean, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; Blue Chalk Sticks is a spreading South African succulent ground cover producing dense clusters of narrow, cylindrical, powder-blue to blue-gray leaves in a distinctive chalk-blue color that provides unique cool-toned contrast in warm-palette Southern landscapes. The intensely blue-gray coloring makes it one of the most visually striking ground covers for sunny, dry gardens and one of the few plants that provides true blue-gray color at the ground level. Stems sprawl and root as they spread, forming a low (12–18 inch tall), dense mat that suppresses weeds and requires virtually no maintenance once established in appropriate conditions. Small white daisy-like flowers appear in summer; the flowers are ornamentally insignificant but produce viable seed. Blue Chalk Sticks is deer-resistant, heat-tolerant, and extraordinarily drought-tolerant once established, making it ideal for xeric, water-wise landscaping in zone 9–12. Propagates very easily from stem cuttings pressed into moist gritty soil. Note: the sap can cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals (like most Senecio species).
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Succulents for California
Cactus and Succulent Society of America
San Diego Cactus and Succulent Society

common_name: Thryallis (Galphimia, Rain of Gold, Golden Thryallis)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Galphimia glauca is one of the most heat-tolerant flowering shrubs for zone 9 Gulf Coast landscapes, blooming continuously from spring through fall and tolerating brief frosts to about 25–28°F when established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Galphimia glauca (commonly called Thryallis, Rain of Gold, or Golden Thryallis) is a Mexican and Central American shrub that is one of the most reliably blooming and low-maintenance flowering shrubs for Gulf Coast and South Texas zone 8–10 landscapes — producing a nearly continuous shower of small, five-petaled, bright golden-yellow flowers on terminal spikes from spring through fall (and sometimes year-round in zone 10+). The effect of hundreds of small golden flowers covering the fine-textured, blue-green foliage is spectacular and sustained, unlike many flowering shrubs that bloom only briefly. Thryallis tolerates heat, humidity, and drought exceptionally well once established, requires minimal fertilization, and is rarely bothered by pests or diseases — the combination of beauty, durability, and low maintenance makes it one of the finest choices for reliable color in challenging Southern landscapes. The shrub grows into a well-behaved, rounded mound (4–8 feet tall and wide over time) without aggressive spreading. Excellent as a specimen, mass planting, or informal hedge. Freezes to the ground in hard winters in zone 8 but resprouts from roots with vigor; essentially evergreen in zone 9b–12.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Shrub Selection for Gulf Coast Landscapes

common_name: Sacha Inchi (Inca Peanut, Mountain Peanut, Star Nut)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and transplant after danger of frost passes; provide a sturdy trellis or support structure (12–15 feet or more) for the vigorous climbing vines. In zone 9, sacha inchi can be grown as a perennial in the warmest sites, dying back in frost but resprouting from roots.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and transplant into well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun with a strong trellis for support; Sacha inchi (also called Inca peanut, star nut, or sacha mani) is a high-altitude Amazonian climbing vine (Plukenetia volubilis, family Euphorbiaceae) native to the cloud forests and montane tropics of Peru, Colombia, and Bolivia, grown for its remarkable star-shaped seed capsules (typically 4-lobed, looking like geometric 4-pointed stars) containing large, oil-rich seeds with an extraordinary nutritional profile — the roasted seeds contain 48–54% oil (the highest of any cultivated edible nut, higher than macadamia) with exceptionally high omega-3 fatty acid content (49% of the oil by weight), making it among the richest plant sources of alpha-linolenic acid. The oil is marketed internationally as a premium cooking and supplement oil with a pleasant, nutty flavor. The seeds are eaten roasted (raw seeds contain alkaloids that are destroyed by roasting) and have a mild, pleasant flavor similar to peanuts. Sacha inchi grows vigorously in humid tropical to subtropical conditions (zones 9b–12) and produces the decorative star-shaped green to brown capsules approximately 6–9 months after planting; the fruits are attractive in the landscape as well as productive.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Sacha Inchi profile
Universidad Agraria La Molina (Peru) — Plukenetia volubilis research publications
